meta.title: Coverage and Spacing Rules — Master Reference
meta.description: Complete spacing, placement, and coverage rules for fire alarm device layout design

spacing_summary_table.description: Quick reference comparison of spacing rules between NFPA 72 and BS 5839

table.notes: BS 5839 spacing is slightly tighter than NFPA for most applications
BS 5839 heat spacing is MUCH tighter than NFPA — significant difference

table.bs5839_spacing: 12.5m from any point to beam, 100m max Tx-Rx distance
table.notes: Both standards require coverage within beam corridor — see beam projection tables

table.bs5839_location: At every exit on every floor
table.notes: BS 5839 travel distance much shorter — more pull stations required

table.nfpa72_spacing: 30 ft (9.1m) on center, within 5 ft (1.5m) of walls
table.bs5839_spacing: 7.5m on center (increased to 11.25m in corridors < 2m wide)
table.notes: Corridor = width ≤ 20 ft (NFPA) or width ≤ 2m with spacing bonus (BS)

table.nfpa72: Supply: downstream of filters. Return: upstream of exhaust
table.notes: Required for HVAC > 2000 CFM (NFPA) or as per risk assessment (BS)

smoke_detector_grid.description: NFPA 72 uses a circular coverage model — detectors spaced on a grid with radius overlap

grid_layout.square: 21 ft × 21 ft maximum grid (6.4m × 6.4m) — ensures corner coverage
grid_layout.notes: The nominal 30 ft spacing is the diagonal measurement. The actual side-by-side spacing on a square grid is 21 ft. This is a common source of error.

corridor_mode.notes: Corridor mode allows wider spacing because walls help contain smoke

ceiling_height_adjustment.description: Spacing must be reduced for high ceilings — smoke stratification prevents full ceiling coverage

correction_table.heat_modifier: Not recommended — use beam or ASD
correction_table.notes: Spot detectors unreliable above 30 ft for smoke

smoke_detector_grid.description: BS 5839 uses a radius model — detector covers a circular area

grid_layout.square: 5.3m × 5.3m maximum grid — but often used as 7.5m × 7.5m triangular spacing
grid_layout.notes: The 7.5m nominal is the maximum center-to-center distance, not the radius. The actual coverage circle radius is 5.3m.

corridor_mode.notes: One of the most important BS 5839 spacing bonuses — commonly used

strobe_spacing_nfpa72.description: Visual notification appliance spacing per NFPA 72 Table 18.5.5.2.1

room_calculation.example: Room 20 ft × 30 ft = 600 sqft → 0.0375 × 600 = 22.5 cd → use 30 cd minimum
room_calculation.notes: If calculated candela exceeds maximum, use multiple devices

sounder_spacing_bs5839.minimum_level: 65 dBA OR 5 dBA above ambient (whichever greater)
sounder_spacing_bs5839.coverage_method: Circular coverage — sounder output must provide minimum level at farthest point in room

typical_spacing.notes: Spacing depends heavily on room acoustics, furnishings, partitions — conservative design recommended

general: Detectors must be on the ceiling or within 300mm of ceiling (BS) / 12 in of ceiling (NFPA)
Do not place detectors within 500mm of HVAC supply diffusers (BS) / 3 ft (NFPA)
Do not place detectors near fluorescent lights (EMI interference)
Do not place detectors where they are obstructed by beams, columns, or ductwork
Maintain access for testing and maintenance (within reach of ladder or platform)
Avoid placing detectors in dead air spaces (corners, ceiling peaks, behind beams)
avoid_areas: Direct path of supply air from HVAC
Near cooking equipment (use heat detector instead)
Bathrooms, showers, steam rooms (moisture causes false alarms)
In direct sunlight (photos may false alarm)
Near diesel generators or exhaust (smoke and fumes)
In dusty or dirty environments (use appropriate EN54 class)
